speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Leopold Zylberman. This is tape 8. We'll take you-- you were just talking about the boat that you came across to Australia in. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- that means that was a freighter with not enough drinking water, you see, or very little. And so we had to take special soap, you see, to-- that's in order to use sea water. And most of the time we spent on the deck. And as I told you before, you see, that means I lost-- one of my last serpents went through the mouth. And you see, we had a fellow with a-- an accordion. And we were singing and listening and watching the dolphins and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the journey last?

speaker: interviewee
text: The journey lasted about 20 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stop off on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowhere. We stopped in Sydney. We arrived in Sydney on the 15th, and we were picked up by the Jewish community, and we were taken to a party, which was sort of strange to us. That means there was a buffet ready, and the hostess made-- bang, bang, bang-- and everybody just went to the tables. They said-- we thought, it's a hungry nation, you see, by chasing the food. But we were showed Sydney. And between Sydney and Melbourne, we already travelled, you see, the passenger class, because every freighter has 12 berth for passengers which, on the way from Hong Kong, were occupied by pregnant women and-- and some people who booked. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your first im--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, while in Sydney, we got familiar with Australian fruit, with everything, and after the American, everything seemed cheap, very cheap. But we didn't know yet how-- how were the Australian wages.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impressions of Australia, landing in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, in my first impression, what I've been looking for is a WC, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you found the WC, what was your impression after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I started looking around, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you see, to find something-- the fellow who brought us to Australia--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kupercyn, Stan Kupercyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please, the surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U-P-E-R-C-Y-N. You see, he picked us up from the port, and he said, listen, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] can you buy some cigarettes? I'd say, no, the canteen is closed. And there was no canteen, actually, because look what we are smoking here. He showed me a zip pouch with tobacco and papers. And I was picked. That means I started going through the customs, and I had already, I bought from an American officer. And they wanted to confiscate it because it wasn't a year old. I declared that it's my private. It wasn't a year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, it is a year old. And he said, why is there no dust inside? And I said, look, sir, that's the only valuable thing in my whole bloody baggage. They said, OK, take it. [LAUGHS] And this was a very valuable asset, because people used to come to me to listen to the news, friends. And it was very dull. I was taken down to Grey Street, St Kilda. How long did you stay in Sydney?

speaker: interviewer
text: Four days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then to Melbourne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were taken to Grey Street, St Kilda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sydney made a very good impression, because those people were-- well, we slept on board the ship. But they picked us up, and they-- they showed us the-- all the [INAUDIBLE] and everything, the port, and they gave us a good meal, you see, after the mutton I ate on the freighter. And then we came to Melbourne. Now, this time you see they asked me about the tobacco. And we went off, and I go through the customs. They said, oh, I didn't know you speak English, the [INAUDIBLE] that you already said very nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, look I haven't got any money. You say, have you got any? I said, well, I had a pound, you see, because I have prepared Australia money, and I knew this-- this fellow, way back from Warsaw. And he took us to the boarding house in Grey Street, St Kilda. And on the way to our room, you see, we saw a couple of dead rats.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it wasn't pleasant, but we were tired, and we went to sleep. And the next morning to-- there was breakfast, you see. That means you could get toast and bacon and eggs and all sorts of things, and we were very happy about it. He took me downtown [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the boarding house, and he-- I bought a hat and a pair of shoes, because I still had money. You see, I came, I had 450 pounds, you see, in a check.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did that money come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: PX, from the job I saved up with the Americans. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then he went into a youth agency and bought me a calling street directory, a little book. And he said, brother, now you're on your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to the boarding house. I said, well, I want a room. And he said, look, rooms are being advertised, but if you want, if you really want a room, you go down to the H at midnight, and when they throw out the first packet of newspapers, you have a pair of scissors, cut the string and take a newspaper and see the room vacant and run to the-- to the place it advertise. Study your street directory well, and knock at the door. I said, look, how could I wake up a landlady. He said, that's-- that's the usual thing, because if you get the second newspaper, you won't get the bloody room. And I did so. I got myself a room at the attic with an Australian girl with an Italian husband-- a big room at the attic, you see. And we have to go to the loo downstairs. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we were very happy. And I was, you see-- after I was sick in Shanghai-- you see, that means I had an infection still after the end of the war. And I lost a lot of weight. And I was sitting in the room, and it was terribly hot. You see, we became terribly hot, and no refrigeration, nothing. You see, if you brought the butter up, you'd see it already was in a liquid. And we were so hungry for sweets and for cheese, that I used to eat Kraft cheese by the briquettes. And then I found out it has the taste of soap, rather. But you know, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you find your first job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't find a job. I came-- well, you see, we met a fellow, you see, who was married to a girlfriend of Lola. You see, they were matriculating together. And he asked me, what can you do? Because a job, you have to by that 7 o'clock, and I wasn't too keen to get, so-- and what can you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him that while we were making the leather baggage things, Lola needed a belt, and I was good in leather, so I copied out of a catalog a belt. Can you make this belt? Yes, how much would you charge? I don't know. How much is-- I'll sell you the leather, and I'll sell you this, and you'll make for me a belt, 100 belts. And that was 8 bob apiece, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so it was 40 pounds, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a little place in 430 Bourke Street. You know, I put up a table and a sewing machine, and I made 100 of those belts. And this fellow put on 100% and sold it to Myer's, you see. So I said, I can do it myself. And so I started a belt factory, you know. And it's a funny thing, you see, I was going, and then I said that I did something in leather for Myer's. But you see, the main business in belts were covered belts for the manufacturers. And a friend of our friends had gave me some work to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to cover belts, and-- and that's where it started, you know. It means, I couldn't manage the electric machine, but somehow I was-- I was doing. And at that time, everything goes. And I went to the Jewish manufacturers to support me, and a fellow I met in a place-- and I spoke to him English. I didn't know he was Jewish. He was a Warsaw Jew. But I met him at the German-- Jewish German office. And I spoke to him English. And then one day, I come to a Jewish customer, a woman, who was running a frock shop, to pick up merchandise. I met him in the hall, and I started to speak English. You see, with him, that's just normal, just like I spoke to everybody in Shanghai and after. And she just walks in, and he said, look at him, he already speaks English. He said, you see when we came, you couldn't stay a word, and this and that and that. And you see, now you can go and go to Australian customers, because we have to support people who can't speak English. And that was a very good tip. [LAUGHS] You see, and then I spent some nine years, and I bought-- that means paid key money for a bigger factory, and I employed 12 girls, and my wife was helping me, you see, in the checking up, and invoicing, and so on. And then a lot of competition came, and we were sick of it, you know. And I sold this, decided to sell the factory and go into sort of a business shop or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, In the meantime, I got-- I wrote to my brother, and he said, look, I didn't want to disturb you while you were making a living in Australia, but now when you sold one business and look for another, why don't you come to Brazil? So I packed up. I went to Brazil, you know, with the little money I had. And I had a very bad impression. I say, that means first of all, we had a very bad journey, because at that time, Lola, they had the baby. Sorry, say that again.

speaker: interviewee
text: We already had a daughter, Ann.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ann.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born on the 15th of October in 1950. And we packed up everything, and she was sick on board ship. We took a ship to Naples, and she was sick on board, and then we had to stop in Italy to Genoa, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we went to [? Navi ?], you see, for her to have a rest. And then Genoa, we boarded a ship, the Augustus, we boarded to go to Santos. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you see, she was, again, sick, you see. And the Italian doctor looked after her or something. And then we came to-- the ship landed, you see, in port, berthed in Santos. And I looked at the thing, and all of the sudden I saw my friends, you see. There was a big company came to Santos, you know. And we came down the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] gangway and was love and everything. And everybody liked Ann because she was very beautiful, and she was young. And we went to the casino, but the casino--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Santos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's Santos?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the port of Sao Paulo state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you met your brother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you met your brother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to-- to meet me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my brother, my sister-in-law, my other sister-in-law, and you see, the kids and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it seeing your brother for the first time after all those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very happy, you see, that means six or seven years. And they-- and I wouldn't go to Brazil, because I knew that he's a bugger. But people said, look, your brother's a millionaire in Brazil, and you sitting here and-- and just scrapping for a living or something. And they simply pushed me on board the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Sao Paulo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too long. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long is too long?

speaker: interviewee
text: About nine months or something. But you see, that means it took two months to go there, and they took us to the casino and have a big thing. And they said it was 100 miles-- 100 kilometers to Sao Paulo, and said that Lola, with the baby, go with Elizabeth, and I will take you. And he took me to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his car. It was an Oldsmobile. And we were driving, and he said-- I said, well-- and Romak was, at that time, in America, in business, so I didn't meet-- didn't meet him. And he said that Romak didn't play fair with him, because I promised him-- he-- he didn't want to-- he wanted a job, and I couldn't afford to give him a job. So I said, look, you will be my English-speaking man, and I'll give you 10%. And he made a lot of money, and I'm not going to make the mistake once again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this day, I saw that I am going back. Because you see, that means he, when Romak was making 10% out of his earning, he was making 90%. And if it is, for Romak, too much money, what can I expect? So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And you see, they were manufacturing plastic, and Romak was 16% in the business, and Henry had 60%, and there were other partners. And Romak proposed that he would give me 3% out of his share. So Henry gives me 7%, and he said, I don't commit the mistake again. Because you see, my daughter has a father-in-law, and I have to support him. And if I take [INAUDIBLE] into the business, so I'll have trouble with my daughter. I said, look, that's the same thing you had the trouble with your wife, because I didn't come to work to you in Warsaw as-- as, you see, as a protege. I brought you the agency of cutting, and I wasn't paid enough either. But she was jealous, because her brother was a messenger boy, and you were an executive. And now, his daughter was jealous. In the meantime, you see, Henry divorced his wife, who later died of cancer, and he was sort of living with a French woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- so you spent nine months in Sao Paulo, and then did you come back to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whereabouts did you go in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was trying different sorts of business, you see, because he said that he doesn't want me in his firm, but he will finance me. And the conditions he gave me-- I said, look, I better go home. But you see, the question is that I had a bit of a fight. You see, they returned to me the status that means, how to have some enough money-- what I left with Australia-- because you see, when I sold the belt business and collected all the thing, I had something like 15,000 pounds, which was money in 1957.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they settled. And in the meantime, [? Robin ?] had an ulcer on his stomach and it was to be operated, and I wanted to be with him. So I sent my wife and Lola and Ann back to Melbourne, and they bunked with friends. And on the way back, you see, after Romak's operation-- everything was all right-- I went to America to look for business.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you see, I had sort of connections, and you see, a fellow who did business with my brother. And I went to the Atlantic City for the weekend, and over there was a meeting exhibition. And I lacked some articles, you see-- elastic. And I knew that at that time, there was import restrictions, and if I sort of can get a couple of machines like that, I can do a living in this. And you see, we were sitting in his place and looking at the Yellow Pages. And we went, instead of buying a new machine, I had to go and buy a secondhand machine from a manufacturer, in the meantime, who would teach me how to do it. And we just rang up a firm, and he was a very smart [INAUDIBLE], a very smart fellow, because he was driving a Cadillac. You see, he was buying second-hand Cadillacs from one of Italian gangsters, or whatever it is, which cost him cheaper than the Buick. And you see, we went up to the thing, and he said, look, I didn't have a dime, you see, to put it in the meter. So we sent a boy from the office, and he said, the Cadillac, and immediately, it was a big prestige.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you end buying this machine and coming back to Australia with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I end up making a partnership.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, that means-- you see, when it came up, I said I-- I have $40,000 I had, you see, out of the 15,000 pounds. You see, that was-- the exchange rate was 25 or something, and I had to establish myself. I have to get a flat or something like this. And we need, you see, capital and thing. We need $60,000 all together, if you do partnership. And they were doing terrific business. And sort of, we talked to each other, liking. And they put in the other money, you know, out of which they bought machines for me. That means I ended with the turnover money out of my own. And I started a business here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, in Richmond.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the main business you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where I saved up for the rest of my life. And you see, we were making material for corsetry, and then I branched out to make a little corset business. I took a working partner, and we're selling fabric plus ready, you see, to about 1,000 dozen girls a week, and I was just on the way to make millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name-- what was the name of the factory that you owned?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Artflex. ?] [? Artflex. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, it sets up with my good luck. You see, somebody invented pantyhose, because most of the girdles were worn to keep the stockings up, you know. And at that time, Lola got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- well, I was sitting in the hospital, and I gave to my two managers, you see, the plenipotentiary or the right to sign, or something like this. And they were very good to me. Your place is not here. Your place is in the hospital. And eventually, you see, the whole business wound up, unfortunately. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I came to my regular thing, and they started-- they presented me with a bill. To think I thought it was charity. It was cunningness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, I was three years on my own, you see. That means I sold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- on your own, business-wise, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that means we were doing, you know-- because you see, other than girdles, we were doing, you see, elastics for Johnson Brothers, and things--

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the years-- in the years that you settled in Australia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the years that you settled in Australia, from '46 until around this period, did you set up a Jewish life for yourself here in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- my friends were all Jews. And we lived in [INAUDIBLE] next door to-- to better [INAUDIBLE] the college. So Ann went to college, and I was taking her to the Sunday school, you see. And I belonged to B'nai B'rith, and all my friends. And in the good old days, I was donating more. Now I reduced my donation, and I live on my savings.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to take a break now and change tapes.
